coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous biosensing of CA125 and CA15-3 tumor markers and imaging of OVCAR-3 and MCF-7 cells lines via bi-color FRET phenomenon using dual blue-green luminescent carbon dots with single excitation wavelength
coredata.dc:creator: Hamd-Ghadareh, Somayeh
Salimi, Abdollah
Parsa, Sara
Fathi, Fardin
coredata.dc:description: Here, we developed a facile route to synthesize two CDs with the maximum emission locates at 326 nm and 443 nm under 230 nm excitation. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) is as high as 29% and 52% for CD-1 and CD-2 with strong blue-green fluorescence, respectively. The functionalized CDs (CDs-NH2, CDs-COOH) conjugated with antibodies used for simultaneous detection of two tumor-markers using fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) strategy, when MoS2 nanosheets functionalized with ssDNA related aptamers applied as quencher. The response of sensor enhanced gradually with increasing of targets at range 0.05–8 pg/mL with detection limit (DL) 5.0 fg/mL for CA125, while for CA15-3 concentration range was 1.0–35 pg/mL and DL was 0.5 pg/mL. This strategy revealed a stable analytical performance for the detection of OVCAR-3 and MCF-7 cells lines ranging from 1000 to 10,000 cells/mL with DL 2–3 cells in 10 μL of injected sample. When the CDs-Ab was used in selective imaging of MCF-7 and OVCAR-3 cancer cells, a strong PL emission was observed. After incubation times, both the cells size and shape clearly remained without any change. The results demonstrate that the CDs are a potentially excellent bioimaging component in pathological diagnosis, biomedicine research and management of cancer diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Carbon dots
MoS2 nanosheets
Simultaneous detection
CA125
CA15-3 tumor markers
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture and confocal fluorescence imaging
Study of the FRET mechanism for the nanoassay
Optical response of FRET nanoassembly toward the simultaneous detection of CA125 and CA15-3 tumor markers
Ability of FRET nanoprobe for tumor markers detection in biological samples
Simultaneous FRET detection of OVCAR-3 and MCF-7 cancer cells
Cellular toxicity and cell imaging of the CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council , (grant number 108129 ) and the Research Offices of the University of Kurdistan (grant number 4.1261 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopic Image of CDs-1 and CDs-2 (A, B), the Gauss fitting of particle size distribution of CDs inset in TEM histogram and the inserted photographs of CDs aqueous solutions under visible (left) and UV (right) light, FTIR spectra of CDs-1 and CDs-2 (C, D), and the typical absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CDs-1 and CDs-2 (E, F).

The Fluorescent spectra of CDs scanned at different excitation wavelengths (A) for CDs-1 and (B) for CDs-2. (C) Normalized fluorescent spectra of CDs-1 and (D) for CD-2. The Fluorescent spectra of the coupled CDs-1 and CDs-2 scanned at different excitation wavelengths. (E) and (F) their related normalized fluorescent spectra.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs-1(red line), CDs-2 (green line), and solution containing both of CDs (black line). (B) Overlapping of emission of both CDs and absorption of MoS2 in ethanol. A photograph of both CDs under UV irradiation is inserted. High-resolution XPS (C), survey scan, (D) C1s, (E), N1 s, (F) spectra for O1s.
The principle of FRET-based sensing platform for simultaneous detection of CA125 and CA15-3 tumor markers.

(A) The fluorescence recovery of CDs-Ab/MoS2-Apta system after incubation with 0.05, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 1.8, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 4.5, 6.0, and 8.0 pg/mL of CA125 (A) and 3.0, 6.5, 7.5, 8.5, 9.0, 10, 13, 16, 25, 33, and 35 spg/mL of CA15-3. (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and concentration of CA125 and CA15-3 (C) and the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and concentration of CA125 in present a certain concentration of CA15-3 (D).

Histogram related to the analytical signal of ssDNA-MoS2/CDs-Ab conjugates after incubation with 5.0 pg/mL of CA125 and 16 pg/mL CA15-3 (a), (a) +3.0 ng/mL AFP (b), (a) +1.5 ng/mL CEA (c), (a) +2.0 ng/mL CA19-9(d), unhealthy serum (d), healthy serum (f).

(A, B) Calibration curves for fluorescence detection of OVCAR-3-cells and MCF-7 cells when simultaneously incubating with CDs-1/Ab and CDs-2/Ab for 10 min followed by incubation with of ssDNA-MoS2 (Concentrations of the cells is noted in related graph).
(A, B) Cellular toxicity assessment of the CDs using CD-1 MTT assay toward OVCAR-3 cells (A) and CDs-2 MTT assay toward MCF-7 (B), (All values are the average of triplicate measurements).

(A, B) Laser scanning microscopy images of OVCAR-3 cells and MCF-7 cells incubated with 0.2 mg/mL CDs at 37 °C, scale bar = 20 μm, typically incubated with CDs/Ab for 2 h and washed cells are imaged under (A) green (b)/yellow (c) red (d) and blue (e) channels, part (a) shows the control cells without CDs-Ab under bright field. (B) Fluorescence microscopy of CDs-2/Ab in the presence of MCF-7 cells when incubating for 2 h under green (b), yellow (c), and blue (d) channels and part (e) shows the blue channel overlay, part (a) shows the control cells without CD-Ab under bright field.
Simultaneous determination of CA 125 and CA 15-3 in serum sample by the proposed immunosensor.
Comparison of analytical performance of various immunosensors for CA 125 and CA 15-3.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous biosensing of CA125 and CA15-3 tumor markers and imaging of OVCAR-3 and MCF-7 cells lines via bi-color FRET phenomenon using dual blue-green luminescent carbon dots with single excitation wavelength

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175 Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175 Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Kurdistan University of Medical Sciences, 66177-13446 Sananandaj, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175 Sanandaj, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we developed a facile route to synthesize two CDs with the maximum emission locates at 326 nm and 443 nm under 230 nm excitation. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) is as high as 29% and 52% for CD-1 and CD-2 with strong blue-green fluorescence, respectively. The functionalized CDs (CDs-NH2, CDs-COOH) conjugated with antibodies used for simultaneous detection of two tumor-markers using fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) strategy, when MoS2 nanosheets functionalized with ssDNA related aptamers applied as quencher. The response of sensor enhanced gradually with increasing of targets at range 0.05–8 pg/mL with detection limit (DL) 5.0 fg/mL for CA125, while for CA15-3 concentration range was 1.0–35 pg/mL and DL was 0.5 pg/mL. This strategy revealed a stable analytical performance for the detection of OVCAR-3 and MCF-7 cells lines ranging from 1000 to 10,000 cells/mL with DL 2–3 cells in 10 μL of injected sample. When the CDs-Ab was used in selective imaging of MCF-7 and OVCAR-3 cancer cells, a strong PL emission was observed. After incubation times, both the cells size and shape clearly remained without any change. The results demonstrate that the CDs are a potentially excellent bioimaging component in pathological diagnosis, biomedicine research and management of cancer diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Simultaneous determination of several biological targets by fluorescence techniques with a single test mode is highly desirable for diseases diagnostics and early cancer recognitions [1, 2]. The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) sensors as distance-dependent signal generating ones generally show signal changes, which are only a few percent of the total signal. The presence of signals not related to the sensor (e.g., cellular autofluorescence) could therefore influence the observable changes in sensor signal and has to be avoided [3, 4]. The complicated and time-consuming operation multiple lasers, are needed when multiple targets have to be detected, this inevitably limit the clinical applications because of the auto fluorescence from the cells and the background noise originating from multiple excitations that are difficult to eliminate [5, 6]. The simultaneous detection can overcome this problem [7, 8]. Homogeneous immunoassays are much in demand, because do not need numerous incubations, washing, and separation steps but only simple mixing and incubation followed by the measurement [9]. In fluorescence simultaneous detections beside its high efficiency of “off” to “on” signals for tracking the targets, the FRET nanoprobe almost does not generate any false-positive signals caused by intrinsic biological interferences [10, 11]. Moreover, FRET allows the simultaneous recording of two emission intensities at different wavelengths in the absence or presence of targets, which can provide a feasible approach for precise detection [12–14].

The critical role of CA125 and CA15-3 for the early detection of ovarian and breast cancers are controversial and have not yet been adopted for widespread screening efforts in asymptomatic women [15]. CA125 to some extent showed limited specificity for ovarian cancer because elevated CA125 levels can be found in individuals without ovarian cancer [16]. For example, while CA125 is best known as a marker for ovarian cancer, it may also be elevated in other cancers, including endometrial cancer, fallopian tube cancer, lung cancer, breast cancer and gastrointestinal cancer. Thus, CA125 testing is not only specific for ovarian cancer but also may for other cancers such as breast cancer, because a single marker due to limited specificity and sensitivity is often not sufficient for diagnosis purpose [17]. Then, multiplexed immunoassay has attracted considerable interest for early screening of disease [18, 19]. In accordance, a series of approaches such as FRET makes it possible for the simultaneous detection of multiple tumor markers with single excitation and multiple emissions. Furthermore, Carbon dots (CDs) are recently recognized as promising candidate nanomaterials in FRET for their potential applications in fields of biosensing and bioimaging that is due to their advantages, such as adjusted emission wavelength, low toxicity, lack of chemical and colloidal instabilities and capable of being used at room temperature [20–26]. The impressive advancements of graphene in a wide range of fields have sparked a strong research interest in a broader family “beyond graphene” [27]. Specially, MoS2 has emerged as one of the thoroughly canonical investigated members of this family; the ultrathin planar structure makes 2D MoS2 first and foremost sensitive to the surrounding environment, where the electrons/holes are restricted to a plane of atomic thickness. The electronic energy transfer (EET) and resonance energy transfer (RET) phenomena of MoS2 nanosheets result in an efficient fluorescence quenching property [28, 29]. Moreover, MoS2 nanosheets were demonstrated to be able to spontaneously adsorb single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) toward double-stranded ones via the van der Waals force between nucleobases and the basal plane of MoS2 nanosheets [30] Following our previous report regarding the application of CDs and FRET in biosensing [31, 32], herein we have designed a sensitive FRET-based nanoprobe for the simultaneous determination of CA125 and CA15-3 tumor markers through a novel strategy. Particular attention will be paid to hybrid nanostructures involving MoS2 and a mixture of two different CDs. In our experiments, we used for the first time a novel coupling of two water-soluble CDs as donors to obtain excellent photoluminescence properties. By varying the ratio of the CDs, these fluorescent nanoparticles exhibit multicolor as well as distinguishable emission signals under single wavelength excitation. FRET as a standard technique for diagnostics is based on energy transfer from CDs-antibody to MoS2-aptamer conjugates through specific recognition of a biomarker by both antibodies (ABs). The fluorescence spectrum band of CDs shows an effective overlap with the absorbance spectrum of MoS2 which affects the detection sensitivity of the immunosensor. After injection of CA125 and CA15-3 antigens as targets to mixture of CDs-Ab probes and MoS2-ssDNA aptamers, the MoS2 released the immunocomplex and the fluorescence signals of CDs recovered again. Furthermore, a self-assembled DNA nanoconstruct for effective imaging of tumor-related markers in living cells based on the FRET “off” to “on” signal readout mode was built through monitoring and tracking OVCAR-3 and MCF-7 cancer cells labeled with CDs barcodes; the results suggest that CDs barcodes are strong candidates for discriminating different labeled cell.

The reagents and methods used to synthesize different materials in this study, and instruments for characterization and fluorescence measuring have been reported in full detail in supporting information (SI) file.

The resorcinol derived CDs were synthesized using an adopted one-step hydrothermal procedure, in a typical experiment, 1.0 g of resorcinol, 400 μL ethylenediamine, and 200 μL H2SO4 were added to 10 mL of water and quickly dissolved to form clean and pale yellow colored homogeneous solution. The mixture then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for a period of 12 h. The room temperature cooled CDs solution was collected by removing the large dots through centrifugation. Then the small carbogenic nanoparticles were obtained after being purified by dialyzing against deionized water through a dialysis membrane for 24 h to remove the excess precursors. The suspension appeared homogeneous saffron and were freeze dried till powder and stored in the dark for further characterization and use. The citric acid derived CDs were synthesized using hydrothermal method [33]. Briefly, in a typical experiment, 1.08 g of citric acid and 335 μL ethylenediamine were added to 10 mL of water and quickly dissolved ultrasonically for 25 min in order to form clean and homogeneous solution. The resulting mixture transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained under autogenous pressure at 180 °C for a period of 12 h. When cooling to room temperature the CDs solution was collected by removing the large dots through centrifugation. For purification the solution was dialyzed against deionized water through a dialysis membrane (1000 MW) for 24 h. The suspension appeared homogeneous dark brown and were freeze dried till powder and stored in the dark for further characterization and use. The CDs conjugated with antibodies for formation of CDs-1-Ab1 and CDs-2-Ab2 reported in in ESI file.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and confocal fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: MCF-7 cells and OVCAR-3 cell lines were cultured in in RPMI 1640 (Gibco) supplemented with 20% fetal bovine serum, 0.01 mg/mL bovine insulin,100 U/mL penicillin, and 100 mg/mL streptomycin (Gibco) at 37 °C in 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere and the cells were seeded in the culture dish.
2D MoS2 nanosheets were obtained based on a one-step hydrothermal reaction by using readily soluble inorganic salts in water. Details of the synthesis process reported in ESI file.

In this case, MCF-7 and OVCAR-3 cells were counted and seeded into 96-well culture plates at a density of 2.5 × 103 cells/well, then incubated for 12 h. After that culture medium was removed and CDs solution in medium with different concentrations, from 10 to 500 μg/mL, were added into each well and incubated for different times from 6 to 48 h and the absorbance was measured at 570/630 nm on Synergy™ HTX Multi-Mode Microplate Reader (Bio Tek, USA) using the Gen5 Microplate Reader and Imager software. MTT assay performed in tetra plicate and repeated three times. Cells in all treated doses in 24 h showed negligible toxicity, higher doses (0.5 mg/mL) showed cytotoxic effect in 48 h, after treatment in (0.1 mg/mL) no significant changes have been seen (means ± SD, n = 3) P value <0.001 and 0.0001 respectively, the cell viability of >90% (OVCAR-3 cells) and >94% (MCF-7 cells) are achieved at a CDs concentration of 80% for MCF-7 cells and >89% for OVCAR-3 cells at a concentration level of 0.2 mg/mL CDs. These results clearly show that functionalized CDs can provide a potential for biological application as nontoxic labels for cellular and in vivo imaging applications.

The size and morphology of the as-prepared CDs was examined by HRTEM. HRTEM images clearly revealed that the synthesized CDs-1 in aqueous medium (Fig. 1.A) were spherical and monodispersed with narrow size distribution of 2–5 nm having the maximum population at 2.5 nm (Fig. 1.A (inset)). The digital photos in Fig. 1A relate to an aqueous solution of CDs-1 under visible light (left) and bright blue luminescence under UV lamp irradiation with a wavelength of 365 nm (right). Similarly, the HRTEM image of CDs-2 is shown in Fig. 1B and the related size distribution diagram is inserted. Based on the particle size distribution histogram in Fig. 1B (inset), it can be seen that the as-synthesized CDs were uniform in size and possess a nearly spherical shape with a mean particle size of approximately 3.2 nm. Photographs of the CDs aqueous solution under natural light (left) and UV lamp of 365 nm (bright green color, right) inserted down side.

The FTIR spectroscopy was utilized to investigate the surface state and chemical structure of the CDs (Fig. 1C, D). The related details reported in SI. Commonly, the optical properties of CDs are known to be governed by a combination of these factors: size, shape and surface state. The UV–vis spectrum showed that the CDs-1 exhibited a significant UV–Vis absorption band centered at approximately 240 nm, that should be attributed to the π-π* transition in the core of CDs and three weaker shoulder peaks at 270–300 nm (Fig. 1E) that is originating from the n–π* transition of aromatic NO bonds [2] The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of the CDs were 230 and 326 nm, respectively. Fig. 1F displayed the characteristic absorption, excitation and emission spectra related to CDs-2. The UV–vis spectrum showed that the CDs have a significant UV–vis absorption band centered at approximately 350 nm, [2, 3] analogous to the optimal fluorescence excitation peak and should be associated to the n-π* transition of the functional groups on the surface of the CDs and a shoulder peak at 180 nm (Fig. 1F) that is a typical characteristic peak of CDs arising from the π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds.

The optimal selected excitation and emission wavelength of the CDs were 345 and 443 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2A, for CDs-1 when the excitation wavelength is varied from 190 to 300 nm, the maximum emission wavelength didn't show any significant fluctuation in the emission. By scanning the excitation wavelength from 310 to 380 nm the variation rhythm in emission and fluorescence intensity is more obvious. Different surface defects can introduce different emission sites to CDs, resulting in variations in the intensities of the emission spectra. Notably, a higher degree of surface oxidation will result in more surface defects, which can trap more excitons. The radiation from the recombination of trapped excitons consequently leads to red-shifted emission. This excitation dependent emission wavelength and fluorescence intensity provide a potential for multicolor imaging applications. The maximum fluorescence emission intensity could be obtained by excitation at 230 nm, with a full width at half-maximum (fwhm) of ca. 74 nm. The recognized excitation-independent emission over 190–310 nm indicates an approximately uniform and well-passivated CDs surface. The normalized PL emission spectra of the CDs-1 with excitation wavelengths from 190 to 380 nm show in Fig. 2C, as it is obvious the emission spectrum of the CDs shift from 326 nm to 430 nm. The related details for PL characterization of CDs-2 in Fig. 2B and D reported in full detail in Supporting information (SI) file. Fig. 2E, F shows the obtained results for simultaneous excitation wavelength scanning of solution containing both CDs. As it is obvious both of them preserve their optical characteristics without any disorder in the maxima or intensity, this phenomenon as one of the novel aspects of CDs characteristics was investigated and exploited in a novel manner in this work.

Different surface defects can introduce different emission sites to CDs, resulting in variations in the intensities of the emission spectra [34]. CDs may, in principle, be separated or manipulated such that some of the inhomogeneity in PL is removed. However, the inhomogeneity may be exploited in the use of surface-passivated CDs for optical labeling to allow the selection of different emission colors. Details of the Quantum yields (QY) measurement, TGA analysis (Fig. S1), PL Stability of CDs toward illumination with a Xe lamp (Fig. S2), pH effect (Fig. S3), and high ionic strengths (Fig. S4) reported in ESI file.

In Fig. 3A (green color) the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-2 indicating a distinguished adsorption peak at 341 nm, which corresponds to the n–π * transition of CO (surface state) bonds and an absorption shoulder located at 180 nm, corresponding to π–π* transitions of CC (core state) bonds [35]. Fig. 3A (black line) shows the absorption spectra related to the mixture of both CDs which shows the characteristics of both of them. Insets in Fig. 3B is the photographs of both CDs dispersions taken under UV lights at the same time. The bright blue and green fluorescent color in the dilute solutions could be observed clearly with the naked eye under a UV lamp. As can be seen in Fig. 3B the UV–vis absorption of MoS2 (centered at 405, 610 and 670 nm) have acceptable overlap with the fluorescence emission band of CDs (centered at 326 and 443 nm), suggesting that an efficient energy transfer between them can take place. In the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy survey of the CDs-1 observed three peaks at 284.0, 401.0 and 533 eV are considered as the C1s, N1s and O1s binding peak, respectively. The XPS survey spectra of the as-prepared CDs (Fig. 3C–F) showed typical peaks at 284.0, 286, 399, 401.0, and 531, 532.6 eV, which indicated that the CDs-1 was mainly composed of, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen atoms [35, 36].

The high resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 3D) indicated the main peaks at 284 and 286 eV, which were attributed to CC, and CO, respectively. The XPS spectrum of N1 s provides a consistent evidence to prove the existence of doping N (Fig. 3E). Interestingly, besides N doping content, the chemical state of N according to the XPS N1s spectrum are assigned to pyridinic C-N-C N1s at 400.00 eV, and 401.56 eV, as pyrrolic C2-N-H, respectively. High N doping tends to form pyridinic N, accompanied with high-luminescence intensity. This could be one of the reasons for fluorescence improvement of N-doped CDs by increasing the nitrogen-doped content. While CDs had a carbon dominant composition (C 67.28%, O 20.21%, N 12.51%), the peak intensities of the oxygen functionalities (OH, CO and COC) is clear (Fig. 3F). The presence of these functional groups located at their surface endowed the CDs with excellent solubility and dispersibility in water. As detailed in Fig. 2B, in the fluorescence emission wavelengths of the CDs-2, for the excitation wavelength over 310–400 nm the PL peak approximately stands consistent (at 443 nm) which is in contrast to the most other luminescent CDs, exhibiting an excitation-dependent PL behavior only when the excitation wavelength is larger than 400 nm. Respectively, by changing the excitation wavelength from 400 to 420 nm, the PL peak shifted from 460 to 490 nm. In contrast, as different energy levels associated with different “surface states” formed by different functional groups are responsible for the excitation-dependent-emission behavior. Fig. 2D shows the normalized PL emission spectra of the CDs with different excitation wavelengths from 190 to 470 nm. It can be seen that the emission spectrum of the CDs changes from 430 nm to 500 nm. The excitation wavelength should be as strong as both of the particles could absorb the enough energy to be excited at the same time, by scanning the excitation wavelength the obtained results showed that 230 nm was adopted as the excitation which can excited the both carbon dots simultaneously with the same brightness, although this wavelength because of its high frequency in ultraviolent region could influence the sensor and may be so strong that cause some damages to biological tissues but by considering this aspect that the homogeneous assays have the factor of high speed of response and so the samples would exposure to the radiation just for a short time this defect would be negligible. The other main advantages of simultaneous determination of biological samples by FRET can overcome the autofluorescence of biological samples. In addition to simultaneous detection, the simplicity and rapidness of diagnostic tests of homogeneous immunoassays are of paramount importance. FRET nanoprobe almost does not generate any false-positive signals caused by intrinsic biological interferences. UV–vis absorbance spectra for the CDs-1 in Fig. 3A (red color) show a relatively strong absorption within 200–400 nm with two characteristic peaks. The XPS survey spectra of the as-prepared CDs (Fig. 3C–F) showed typical peaks at 284.0, 286, 399, 401.0, and 531, 532.6 eV, which indicated that the CDs-1 was mainly composed of, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen atoms [33].

The sonication assisted hydrothermal synthesis technique is a powerful way to break the probably weak interlayer forces in as synthesized materials. Fig. S5 in ESI (A–D) show the low and high (Fig. S1. C Inset) FESEM images of MoS2 nanosheets. The images show the lateral view of some sheet-like morphology with visible lattice fringes and it confirms the formation of MoS2 nanosheets. More related details reported in ESI (Fig. S1A–D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the FRET mechanism for the nanoassay
ce:section.ce:para: The nanoprobes consist of two CDs (include resorcinol-derived CDs and citric acid-derived one, named CDs-1 and CDs-2) functionalized by CA125 (CDs-1) and CA15-3 Abs (CDs-2) which act as the fluorescent probe (FRET donor) and aptamers were stabilized on the surface of MoS2 nanosheets (FRET acceptor). When the single emission CDs-1 (with emission at 326 nm) was mixed with the other CDs-2 (wavelength emission at 443 nm), independently two emission peaks at 326 nm and 443 nm without any interfering effects in shape and maxima positions were observed and it could be compared with the case for bi-color or donor-donor FRET nanoprobe, suggesting that the collected signal at these two emission wavelengths are related to both of CDs with single wavelength excitation. To test the feasibility of simultaneously detect and image two tumor targets using single excitation, MoS2 nanosheets were chosen as the receptors for the nanoprobe, because they can be employed as efficient quenchers and can also be modified with many oligonucleotides. The fluorescent probe would be adsorbed on the surface of MoS2 nanosheets due to van der Waals force between nucleobases and the basal plane of MoS2 nanosheets. The absorption of CDs onto MoS2 leads to fluorescence quenching of CDs. After being excited with a single wavelength, in the presence of MoS2 conjugated aptamers, the fluorescence of the corresponding fluorophores can all be changed and the FRET will occur between multiple fluorophores. The details of this approach are described in Fig. 4 . The FRET nanoprobe was simply prepared by two customized single stranded oligonucleotide and two Ab counter markers (CA125 and CA15-3 aptamers and CA125 and CA15-3 antibodies). The designed aptamers were to have a target recognition site and were modified with MoS2 nanosheets, respectively. In the absence of a targets, the donor fluorophore (CDs-1 and CDs-2) and 2D MoS2 receptor were self-assembled, thus, FRET was in the “on” state. However, the presence of targets resulted in target-induced configurational changes of the FRET nanoprobe, shifting FRET from “on” to “off” state, by spatially separating the donor and acceptor. Successful formation of the FRET nanoprobe was certified by following the changes in nanoprobe fluorescence feedbacks. Such a FRET immunosensor provides an ideal distance dependent signal readout by an excellent quenching efficiency between CDs-labeled Ab and MoS2-stabilized aptamer via electrostatic interaction, the experimental evidence of aptamer conjugation to MoS2 reported in ESI file (Fig. S6). The MoS2 have strong absorption bands and are able to quench several emission wavelengths simultaneously.

Upon the addition of tumor targets to the CDs-MoS2 system, the fluorescence emission was restored, which means aptamer can release the adsorbed MoS2 from the surface of CDs because of the stronger coordination ability of the aptamer with tumor targets compared to that of the electrostatic interaction. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of simultaneous determination of tumor markers two independent CDs at a single excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical response of FRET nanoassembly toward the simultaneous detection of CA125 and CA15-3 tumor markers
ce:section.ce:para: Since our nanoprobe was demonstrated to have good target-binding ability, its response to the target markers was explored in the following fluorescence response experiments. As shown in Fig. 5A–C, the fluorescence increased with increasing of CA125 concentration in the range of 50.0 fg/mL to 8.0 pg/mL with regression of R2 = 0.9965, while for CA15-3 target the fluorescence response increased with increasing of CA15-3 at the concentration range of 1.0 pg/mL to 35.0 ng/mL and R2 = 0.992. The calculated detection limit of the proposed method was 5.0 fg/mL (3 S/N) for CA125 and 0.5 pg/mL for CA15-3. A significant amplification of fluorescence response could be clearly observed, which could be attributed to the increased nanoprobe spatial distance from the quencher and more sandwich hybrid construction. Also the results related to biosensing of CA125 target (0.5 to 8.0 pg/mL) in the presence of a constant concentration of CA15-3 Ag (16.0 pg/mL) showed in Fig. 5D, clearly testified the selectivity of the nanostructure response toward the markers. In Table 1B. there is a comparison of analytical performance of various immunosensors for CA 125 and CA 15–3. An almost 4.2-folds increase in F/F0 signal was observed when the concentration of targets increased. Furthermore, the signal response for different concentration of CA15-3 in the presence of constant concentration value of CA125 was investigated (Fig. S7). The results demonstrated that the interference effect of CA125 on the CA15-3 signal response is negligible. In general, coupling complementary has been accepted as a principal rule in biological systems. This is done by separately setting the complementary aptamers of CD-1 and CD-2 loaded antibodies on MoS2 nanosheets respectively. These locations afford interactions in the form of significant binding affinity interactions which can be regarded as selectivity hot spots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ability of FRET nanoprobe for tumor markers detection in biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: The greatest problem when designing FRET nanoprobe for complex biological systems has always stemmed from intrinsic interferences that arise from such matrices, including false-positive signals raised from other proteins. In order to demonstrate the ability of our proposed FRET nanoprobe, the interaction of FRET with other cancer markers, unhealthy (positive) serum and healthy (negative) serum samples was studied. As disclosed in Fig. 6(a–f), the response of the sensor toward (5.0 pg/mL CA125 and 16.0 pg/mL CA15-3) (a) and samples containing (5.0 pg/mL CA125 and 16.0 pg/mL CA15-3) and (AFP, 3.0 ng/mL) (b), samples containing (5.0 pg/mL CA125 and 16.0 pg/mL CA15-3) (CEA, 1.50 ng/mL) (c), and samples containing (5.0 pg/mL CA125 and 16.0 pg/mL CA15-3) (CA19-9, 2.0 ng/mL) (d) was tested under optimum conditions. The results showed the high specificity of the probe toward the target markers without any serious interferences. We found the concentration of CA125Ag and CA15-3 antigens in patient serum sample 4.30 ± 5.0 pg/mL, and 6.20 ± 5.0 pg/mL which was in good agreement with reported values based on ELISA method in clinical laboratory (4.38 ng/mL for CA125 and 6.29 pg/mL for CA15-3) (Fig. 6, part e). The application of the immunosensor for simultaneous detection of CA125 and CA15-3 unhealthy serum samples was evaluated and the obtained results by the immunosensor (with consideration of the dilution factors) were compared with that of the analogous samples measured by the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kit. Two serum samples containing different amounts of proposed target markers were used to evaluate the applicability of this FRET immunoassay for real samples analysis. As shown in Table 1A , the measured values by proposed immunosensor are in good agreement with reported results by ELISA as reference method. Furthermore, the human serum samples were spiked with 1.0 and 0.1 pg/mL of CA 125 and CA 15–3 for indicating the accuracy of results, and the concentration of tumor markers was calculated from the related calibration graph. As displayed in Table 1A, the recoveries and the corresponding RSD showed satisfactory results. These indicated that the constructed FRET immunosensor can be successfully used for simultaneous determination of CA 125 and CA 15-3 in human serum samples in clinical diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous FRET detection of OVCAR-3 and MCF-7 cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: OVCAR-3 cells and MCF-7 cells contained overexpressed molecular target MUC16 protein and cytokeratin 8 (CK8) on their surface respectively, which led to the CA125-Ab and CA15-3-Ab attaching to the cell surface through a specific interaction between the Ab to MUC16 and CK8. The attaching of the CA125-Ab to OVCAR-3 and CA15-3 to MCF-7 cells was confirmed by the fluorescence characterization of the cells. Based on the ascribed sensing protocol, by using the affinity concept there is a strong affinity between antibody and the correlated antigen. The OVCAR-3 and MCF-7 cells here as the model cell lines have the rule of antigen in fact. The overexpressed protein or CK8 on cell surface play the antigen role and lead to performing a highly selective affinity based interaction between antibodies and the counter cells. So the CDs modified by antibody show specific affinity toward OVCAR-3 or MCF-7 cells.

Fig. 7A, B, show the calibration curves for fluorescence detection of OVCAR-3 and MCF-7 cells when simultaneously incubating with CDs-1/Ab and CDs-2/Ab for 10 min followed by incubation with ssDNA-MoS2. The fluorescence intensity was proportional to the cell number of OVCAR-3 and MCF-7 cells (Fig. 7A) when cells concentration range increased from 1000 to 10,000 cell/mL simultaneously and the linear regression equation was y(FL) = 13.485x + 419.93 (R2 = 0.9974), y(FL) = 29.976x + 257.93 (R2 = 0.9932), respectively, where x is the cell number. The calculated limit of detection (LOD) was 200 cell/mL (2 cells in 10 μL of injected sample) and 300 cell/mL (3 cells in 10 μL of injected sample) for OVCAR-3 and MCF-7 cells, respectively, at S/N = 3. This result suggested that FRET could perform excellent self-delivery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular toxicity and cell imaging of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As toxicity always is a critical factor to evaluate the factual practicality of the arranged CDs in cell imaging, the characteristic cell cytotoxicity of our synthesized CDs was evaluated by treating CDs-1 with OVCAR-3 and CDs-2 with MCF-7 cells at the concentration level of 10–500 μg/mL as a test sample by a standard colorimetric MTT assay. The experimental results in Fig. 8 A showed the toxicity assay for 0.2 mg/mL of CDs-1 toward OVCAR-3 cells at different times and Fig. 8 B indicated cytotoxicity toward MCF-7 cells for low doses (0.1 mg/mL), medium, (0.25 mg/mL), and high doses (0.5 mg/mL) of CDs-2 at different time intervals.

Fig. 9A, B illustrates the Laser scanning microscopy images of OVCAR-3 cells and MCF-7 cells incubated with 0.2 mg/mL CDs at 37 °C, the OVCAR-3 cells treated with CDs-1s and MCF-7 cells incubated with CD-2 showed no significant change in shape of the treated cells. In Fig. 9A, a strong green (b), yellow (c), red (d) and blue (e) colored photoluminescence on the cell membrane and cytoplasmic area of cells indicated that the CDs entered into the cells and conserved their fluorescent characteristics in the cellular environment, while no photoluminescence is observed at nucleus. Part (a) shows the control cells without CD-Ab under bright field.

Fig. 9B shows the fluorescence microscopy of CDs-2/Ab in the presence of MCF-7 cells when incubating for 2 h under green (b), yellow (c), and blue (d) channels and part (e) shows the blue channel overlay. It is clear from Fig. 9A, B that the rod-shaped OVCAR-3 cells can be homogeneously and successfully labeled by CDs. Also no morphological changes of the rounded shape human breast cancer cell line MCF-7 after treatment with trial (0.2 mg/mL) was observed. Panel a, is negative control group, some cell debris was observed in the medium. The results seem to suggest that the cellular uptake of CDs is efficient, requiring only a short incubation time (2−3 h). As shown in Fig. 8A, B the results of cellular toxicity assessment of the CDs using CDs-1 MTT assay toward OVCAR-3 cells (E) and CDs-2 toward MCF-7(F) exhibited no cytotoxicity or side effects in living cells. These results confirmed that the FRET nanoprobe could be potentially used for the intracellular detection of specific tumor markers. The detailed results related to selectivity of the imaging protocol reported in ESI file (Figs. S8–S11).

Collectively, we report a novel approach to construct FRET based nanoprobes through aptamer-protein marker self-assembly for highly efficient and reliable simultaneous detection of CA125 and CA15-3 tumor-related markers by amine/carboxylate functionalized CDs sensors in homogeneous solution. The designed FRET nanoprobe composed of both CDs and 2D MoS2 showed excellent applicability in complicated biological matrixes for highly selective and sensitive simultaneous detection of tumor markers such as CA125 and CA15-3. Also, architecture FRET nanoprobes were employed to differentiate cancer cells from normal cells and moreover, for cell counting the fluorescence intensity is correlated positively with the number of cells in a concentration-dependent manner with detention limit of 2–3 cells in 10 μL of injected sample. The FRET nanoprobe bolded many superior advantages, such as facile synthesis, simultaneous-labeling of proteins markers and oligonucleotide with biocompatible, single wavelength excitation of both CDs, low cytotoxicity and cost-effective fluorophores, which provide an option for efficient simultaneous monitoring for targets of interest. The brilliant physical properties combined with the outstanding advantages of FRET “off” to “on” signal readout beside the low detection limit, desirable selectivity and rapid target response rhythm indicate that this FRET nanoprobe has the potential for further application in detecting tumor-related markers in biomedicine research, pathological diagnosis and management of cancer diseases. Although our finding suggests that the synergistic combination of the probe and complementary loaded nanoparticles at the hot spots contribute significantly toward molecular selection and recognition. Our results obtained in this study can have significant implications to the affinity based binding in biomolecular systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council, (grant number 108129) and the Research Offices of the University of Kurdistan (grant number 4.1261).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2018.06.116.
